text: A farmer must transport a fox, a chicken, and grain across a river. The boat fits the farmer plus one item. Fox eats chicken; chicken eats grain if left alone. What must go FIRST?
explanation: Chicken must go first — it conflicts with BOTH others. Fox first → chicken eats grain ✗. Grain first → fox eats chicken ✗. Chicken first → fox and grain coexist ✓.

text: 3 missionaries and 3 cannibals must cross a river (boat holds 2). If cannibals outnumber missionaries on either bank (with missionaries present), they're eaten. Minimum one-way trips?
explanation: 11 trips. The key constraint: 0 missionaries on a bank is safe. Critical moves involve sending cannibals ahead, bringing one back, ferrying missionaries, then shuttling remaining cannibals.

text: Two unmarked jugs: 3L and 5L. Using only these and a water source, minimum fill/pour/empty operations to measure exactly 4 liters?
explanation: 6 steps: 1) Fill 5L. 2) Pour 5→3 (5L=2, 3L=3). 3) Empty 3L. 4) Pour 5→3 (5L=0, 3L=2). 5) Fill 5L. 6) Pour 5→3 until full (5L=4, 3L=3). Result: 4L in the 5L jug.

text: Farmer, fox, chicken, grain — boat fits farmer + one item. Fox eats chicken; chicken eats grain if alone. Minimum one-way boat trips to get everything across?
explanation: 7 trips: 1) Take chicken →. 2) Return ←. 3) Take fox →. 4) Bring chicken back ←. 5) Take grain →. 6) Return ←. 7) Take chicken →. The critical insight: you MUST bring the chicken BACK on trip 4.
